options.B: She submitted an essay draft for review.
options.C: She fixed the formatting of her citations.

options.B: To ask for information about the timeline for finishing her work
options.C: To find out Professor Mercer's expectations for the final version
options.D: To request permission to use a non-standard citation style

options.B: It features the largest central square in northern Europe.
options.C: It is famous for its art museums.

options.A: One who enjoys meeting local people
options.B: One who likes to hike in nature
options.C: One who wants to learn about Estonian history

options.A: attending a service at St. Olaf's Church
options.C: learning to speak some words in the local language
options.D: sampling the local cuisine at one of the markets

paragraphs: Solid mechanics, crucial in the design of bridges, examines the forces acting on materials to ensure structures withstand traffic and environmental stressors. Effective load distribution is essential, not only to balance static loads, such as the weight of the bridge itself and of any stationary objects on it, but also to manage dynamic loads caused by wind, earthquakes, and moving traffic. Truss bridges demonstrate this principle through triangular frameworks that efficiently channel forces across the structure.

Over time, material selection has evolved to meet increasing demands. Stone and wood, once common, are limited by low tensile and compressive strength, making them unsuitable for long spans or heavy loads. Modern bridges rely on steel and reinforced concrete: Steel resists stretching forces (tensile loads), while concrete is a durable composite capable of withstanding compression and handling complex stress patterns.

Suspension bridges integrate these materials and use these concepts to achieve unprecedented spans. High-strength steel cables transfer loads to vertical towers and anchorages, allowing the deck to remain flexible yet stable. The Golden Gate Bridge in San Francisco, California, completed in 1937, exemplifies this innovation with its 1,280-meter main span. Suspension bridges are particularly effective in regions prone to dynamic forces, as their design allows movement while maintaining structural integrity.

questions.question: The passage mentions all of the following as important considerations for bridge designers EXCEPT

options.B: the weight of vehicles that will cross the bridge
options.C: the type of vehicles moving across the bridge
options.D: environmental stressors such as wind and earthquakes

questions.question: What does the passage suggest about bridges made of stone or wood?

options.A: They are more difficult to build because the materials are so heavy.
options.B: They are limited to shorter lengths than steel and concrete bridges are.
options.C: They continue to be built in regions not prone to strong dynamic forces.
options.D: They do not require as much maintenance as steel and concrete bridges do.

questions.question: The word "composite" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.special_content: Steel resists stretching forces (tensile loads), while concrete is a durable <mark>composite</mark> capable of withstanding compression and handling complex stress patterns.

questions.question: Identify the sentence in paragraph 3 that describes the structure of a suspension bridge.
questions.answer: High-strength steel cables transfer loads to vertical towers and anchorages, allowing the deck to remain flexible yet stable.

questions.question: Why does the author describe the Golden Gate Bridge?

options.A: To illustrate the effectiveness of a specific engineering approach
options.B: To give historical background on bridge construction in California
options.C: To suggest that California is a difficult place to construct bridges
options.D: To provide an example of a suspension bridge that is overly flexible

questions.special_content: The <mark>Golden Gate Bridge</mark> in San Francisco, California, completed in 1937, exemplifies this innovation with its 1,280-meter main span.

paragraphs: Class size remains one of the most debated variables in education. Studies consistently show that smaller classes can enhance student learning by enabling more individualized attention and reducing classroom disruptions. For example, a 2025 study found that reduced student-to-teacher ratios tend to improve academic performance. However, the magnitude of these gains is often modest and accompanied by substantial financial costs.

Of course, academic performance is not the only factor affected by class size. Classroom dynamics, student engagement, and students' satisfaction with their experience are also affected. Researchers Wang and Calvano argue that evidence about the effects of class size remains incomplete because many investigations focus narrowly on academic outcomes rather than broader educational experiences.

Many regions have implemented class-size reduction initiatives, but fiscal pressures have forced reconsideration of these policies. Given the financial constraints on schools, a nuanced approach seems advisable. Research has shown that reducing student-to-teacher ratios in the early years of education, such as in preschool and primary levels, and in higher poverty areas, as well as implementing strategies like small-group tutoring, offer a preferable cost-benefit ratio compared to general class size reduction.

questions.question: The passage indicates which of the following about "classroom disruptions"?

options.A: They tend to have a greater impact on less experienced teachers.
options.B: They have less impact on academic outcomes than does class size.
options.C: They sometimes increase in frequency when class sizes are reduced.
options.D: They can be decreased by having fewer students in a class.

questions.special_content: Studies consistently show that smaller classes can enhance student learning by enabling more individualized attention and reducing <mark>classroom disruptions</mark>.

questions.question: The author mentions "the magnitude of these gains" primarily to

options.A: support an approach to improve academic outcomes
options.B: point out limitations to a statement about the effect of class size
options.C: cite a factor that a research study failed to measure
options.D: acknowledge that a study's findings appear contradictory

questions.special_content: However, <mark>the magnitude of these gains</mark> is often modest and accompanied by substantial financial costs.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the relationship between paragraph 1 and paragraph 2?

options.A: Paragraph 2 summarizes competing arguments on the topic introduced in paragraph 1.
options.B: Paragraph 2 confirms a widely held view mentioned in paragraph 1.
options.C: Paragraph 2 presents evidence that contradicts the findings discussed in paragraph 1.
options.D: Paragraph 2 points out that the findings presented in paragraph 1 are not complete.

questions.question: The passage identifies each of the following as an effect of reducing class sizes EXCEPT:

options.B: Students can receive more individualized attention.

questions.question: The word "nuanced" in the passage is closest in meaning to
questions.special_content: Given the financial constraints on schools, a <mark>nuanced</mark> approach seems advisable.
